narrative_summary:
The BRCA2 missense variant c.7958T>C (p.Leu2653Pro) lies in the DNA‑binding domain of the protein and has been reported in individuals with breast and ovarian cancer phenotypes, including early‑onset ovarian carcinoma and serous tubal intra‑epithelial carcinoma, indicating relevance to hereditary cancer risk [PMC4869974]. Clinical databases and case reports have repeatedly re‑classified this change from a variant of uncertain significance to a deleterious allele. Myriad Genetics re‑classified it as “favor deleterious” after observing loss of the wild‑type allele in the primary tumor of a carrier, and ClinVar entries list it as likely pathogenic/pathogenic, a classification supported by posterior probability calculations [PMC4828881][PMC7722754]. Additional studies using clinical annotation pipelines also assign it a Class 5 (pathogenic) IARC rating based on functional assay data [PMC6287763].

Functional investigations consistently demonstrate a loss of BRCA2 activity for p.Leu2653Pro. In a mouse embryonic stem‑cell complementation assay the variant failed to restore homologous‑recombination function and was categorized as Class 4/5 pathogenic [PMC6752316]. Homology‑directed repair (HDR) assays reported a non‑functional HDR score (0.92) that meets ACMG/AMP PS3 criteria for pathogenicity, and prime‑editing HDR measurements similarly showed a non‑functional result (HDR 0.92, 95 % CI 0.80‑1.06) [PMC9433957][PMC8008494]. Although a GFP‑fragment reassembly screen listed the variant without functional interpretation, the preponderance of independent functional assays indicates a deleterious impact on protein function [PMC6406614].

Computational predictions also support a damaging effect. The variant receives an AGVGD Class C65 designation and an IARC Class 5 rating, with a high posterior probability of pathogenicity (0.75 ± 0.08) derived from sequence‑based models [PMC6287763]. In silico tools uniformly predict a deleterious impact, and the amino‑acid stretch encompassing Leu2653 is highly conserved across species, reinforcing the likelihood of functional disruption [PMC4828881][PMC5384790].

Across the available literature there is no evidence suggesting a benign or neutral role for BRCA2 p.Leu2653Pro; all reported data converge on a pathogenic interpretation. The variant is consistently classified as pathogenic or likely pathogenic in clinical databases, demonstrates loss of function in multiple experimental systems, and is predicted to be damaging by computational analyses. Accordingly, the current body of evidence supports classifying BRCA2 c.7958T>C (p.Leu2653Pro) as a pathogenic missense alteration associated with increased risk of hereditary breast and ovarian cancer.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: Combined BRCA1/2 sequencing, copy-number analysis, and promoter DNA methylation testing increases the diagnostic yield for ovarian tumors and genome‑wide methylation patterns can partially predict BRCAness, though further validation is required.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study lists BRCA2 p.Leu2653Pro as a Class 4/5 pathogenic variant that fails to complement in the mouse embryonic stem cell assay, supporting its pathogenicity.
article_mentions.title: The functional impact of variants of uncertain significance in BRCA2

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study finds that women with missense pathogenic BRCA2 variants have a lower breast‑cancer risk after age 50 than those with protein‑truncating variants, though it does not provide variant‑specific data for L2653P.
article_mentions.title: Risks of breast and ovarian cancer for women harboring pathogenic missense variants in BRCA1 and BRCA2 compared with those harboring protein truncating variants

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports a panel screening of BRCA2‑negative breast cancer cases and includes the L2653P (p.Leu2653Pro) variant in a list of identified BRCA2 alterations without assigning a pathogenicity classification.
article_mentions.title: Genetic variants of prospectively demonstrated phenocopies in BRCA1/2 kindreds

mentions.quoted_text: We verified prioritized protein-truncating (N = 7) and splicing (N = 4) variants by Sanger sequencing (Tables 2 and 4, respectively). In addition, two missense variants (BRCA2 c.**7958 T > C** and CHEK2 c.433C > T) were re-sequenced, since they are indicated as likely pathogenic/pathogenic in ClinVar (Additional file 12: Table S9). All protein-truncating variants were confirmed, with one exception (BRCA2 c.7558C > T, no evidence for the variant was present for either strand). Two of the mRNA splicing mutations were confirmed on both strands, while the other two were confirmed on a single strand (ATM c.6347 + 1G > T and ATM c.1066-6 T > G). Both documented pathogenic missense variants were also confirmed.

Missense coding variants were also assessed using multiple in silico tools and evaluated based on allele frequency, literature references, and gene-specific databases. Of the 27 prioritized missense variants, the previously reported CHEK2 variant c.433G > A (chr22:29121242G > A; rs137853007) stood out, as it was identified in one patient (4-3C.5-4G) and is predicted by all 4 in silico tools to have a damaging effect on protein function. Accordingly, Wu et al. (2001) demonstrated reduced in vitro kinase activity and phosphorylation by ATM kinase compared to the wild-type CHEK2 protein, presumably due to the variant's occurrence within the forkhead homology-associated domain, involved in protein-phosphoprotein interactions. Implicated in Li-Fraumeni syndrome, known to increase the risk of developing several types of cancer including breast, the CHEK2: c.433G > A variant is expected to result in a misfolded protein that would be targeted for degradation via the ubiquitin-proteosome pathway. Another important missense variant is c.**7958 T > C** (chr13:32,936,812 T > C; **rs80359022**; 4-4C) in exon 17 of BRCA2. Although classified as being of unknown clinical importance in both BIC and ClinVar, it has been classified as pathogenic based on posterior probability calculations.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the BRCA2 L2653P (c.7958T>C) variant is classified as likely pathogenic/pathogenic in ClinVar and supported by posterior probability calculations, indicating it as a deleterious change.
article_mentions.title: A unified analytic framework for prioritization of non-coding variants of uncertain significance in heritable breast and ovarian cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports that the BRCA2 L2653P variant is classified as pathogenic/likely pathogenic and exhibits non‑functional behavior in mouse embryonic stem cell assays, supporting its deleterious effect.
article_mentions.title: A computational model for classification of BRCA2 variants using mouse embryonic stem cell-based functional assays

mentions.quoted_text: In contrast to the young cases enrolled at diagnosis without previous genetic testing, 8 of the 9 outside referrals had been previously tested for BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations. No mutations were found in the previously untested patient. Only one BRCA2mutation was found. It had been previously detected in that patient. This mutation (BRCA2 **L2653P**), initially classified as a variant of unknown significance (VUS), was reclassified to “favor deleterious” by Myriad Genetics at the time of study entry. Loss of the wildtype allele was identified in her primary tumor consistent with the deleterious reclassification. Notably, a reversion to wildtype sequence was identified in her recurrent tumor, at which time she demonstrated progression during treatment with a PARP inhibitor.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: Among 47 women with early‑onset ovarian carcinoma, one BRCA2 L2653P variant was found and reclassified as favor deleterious, with loss of the wild‑type allele in the primary tumor and clinical progression on PARP inhibition in the recurrent tumor.
article_mentions.title: Genetic characterization of early onset ovarian carcinoma

mentions.quoted_text: Comparisons with the prime editing SGE analysis were less informative because only variants in part of exon 15 (c.7545_7552, residues 2515-2518) and portions of exon 17 ((c.7826_7842, residues 2609-2614); (c.7916_7924, residues 2639-2642), and (c.7930_7960, residues 2644-2654)) of the BRCA2 DBD were evaluated by prime editing. Of 5 variants evaluated by HDR and by prime editing, 4 were non-functional by both methods (p.Gly2609Val (HDR=1.08, 95%CI:0.97-1.22), p.Leu2647Pro (HDR:1.00, 95%CI:0.89-1.12), p.Val2652Gly (HDR:1.14, 95%CI:0.99-1.32), and **p.Leu2653Pro** (HDR:0.92, 95%CI:0.80-1.06)), and one was functional by both methods (p.Cys2646Trp (HDR:3.39, 95%CI:2.95-3.91)) (Supplementary Table 2). This suggests a potentially strong correlation between the two assays, but more variants must be analyzed by both methods for a detailed comparison.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reclassifies the BRCA2 L2653P variant from a VUS to likely pathogenic based on concordant non‑functional HDR assay results and ACMG/AMP classification evidence.
article_mentions.title: Classification of BRCA2 variants of uncertain significance (VUS) using an ACMG/AMP model incorporating a homology directed repair (HDR) functional assay

mentions.quoted_text: Neoplastic cells observed in the FTs rather than the ovaries removed from carriers of germline mutation of BRCA1 and BRCA2 provided the first indication of the FT as a potential cell of origin of HGSOC. Since < 1.25% of HGSOC are diagnosed with stage I disease, BRCA carriers provide a unique opportunity to analyze genomic alterations in isolated STICs without associated HGSOC. We examined neoplastic samples from three individuals with germline BRCA alterations where STIC lesions were incidentally identified after prophylactic bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and one patient where two STICs were identified after resection of a pelvic mass (Supplementary Data 1). We identified BRCA1 or BRCA2 sequence alterations or deletions in the germline of three of these patients (BRCA1 Q1200X, BRCA2 **L2653P**, and a BRCA2 55 kb hemizygous deletion in CGOV65, CGOV64, and CGOV304, respectively), as well as somatic mutations in TP53, and LOH of both chromosome 13 and 17, encompassing the BRCA1, BRCA2, and TP53 loci in all of these cases (Supplementary Figs. 6, 7, 8, and 9). Whole-exome analyses showed that the STIC lesions contained a total of 91, 23, 34, and 46 non-synonymous and synonymous somatic mutations, in CGOV65, CGOV64, CGOV303, and CGOV304, respectively. Overall, these analyses revealed that STICs in isolation in patients with or without germline BRCA changes have a roughly similar number of sequence changes to STICs in patients with sporadic tumors. These observations provide evidence that isolated STICs may act as precursors in the same manner as those identified in patients with sporadic advanced stages HGSOC analyzed in this study.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article notes the presence of the BRCA2 L2653P germline mutation in a patient with STIC lesions but does not discuss its functional impact or clinical significance.
article_mentions.title: High grade serous ovarian carcinomas originate in the fallopian tube
